author: Dr. Jaswin D'Souza
excerpt: Discover what sinusitis is, why it happens, and how to treat it. From symptoms and causes to home remedies and surgery, here's everything you need to know.
title: Understanding Sinusitis: Causes, Symptoms & Treatment
bodyHtml:
If you've ever had a cold that just wouldn't quit — with that heavy, pressure-filled feeling behind your eyes and cheeks — you may have experienced sinusitis. It's one of the most common conditions we see at our ENT clinic, and yet it's often misunderstood or left untreated for too long. In this post, we'll walk you through everything you need to know about your sinuses, what happens when they become inflamed, and how to find relief.

What Are the Sinuses — and What Do They Do?

Your sinuses are a connected system of hollow, air-filled cavities located within the bones of your skull and face. There are four pairs of sinuses in total:

  • Maxillary sinuses — the largest pair, located in your cheekbones
  • Frontal sinuses — situated in the forehead, just above the eyes
  • Ethmoid sinuses — a cluster of small cavities between the eyes and nose
  • Sphenoid sinuses — located deep behind the nose, near the base of the skull

These cavities are lined with a thin layer of mucous membrane that produces mucus — the fluid that keeps your nasal passages moist, traps dust and pathogens, and helps filter the air you breathe. The sinuses also help humidify and warm incoming air, reduce the weight of the skull, and contribute to the resonance of your voice. When they're working well, you barely notice them. When they're not, you certainly do.

What Is Sinusitis?

Sinusitis — also called rhinosinusitis — is the inflammation or swelling of the tissue lining the sinuses. When the sinuses become blocked and fill with fluid, bacteria, viruses, or fungi can grow and cause an infection. The result is that familiar feeling of facial pressure, congestion, and general misery.

Acute Sinusitis

Acute sinusitis comes on suddenly and typically lasts up to four weeks. It most commonly follows a cold or upper respiratory infection. Most people recover fully with appropriate treatment or even on their own, though some cases require medical attention.

Chronic Sinusitis

Chronic sinusitis is diagnosed when symptoms persist for 12 weeks or longer, despite treatment attempts. It's a more complex condition that often involves ongoing inflammation rather than a straightforward infection. People with chronic sinusitis may experience milder but persistent symptoms that significantly affect their quality of life — disrupting sleep, concentration, and daily comfort.

Common Causes of Sinusitis

Sinusitis rarely has a single cause. Several factors can trigger or contribute to sinus inflammation:

Viral and Bacterial Infections

The most common trigger for acute sinusitis is a viral upper respiratory infection — in other words, the common cold. The virus causes the nasal lining to swell, blocking the sinus drainage pathways. In some cases, bacteria then take hold in the stagnant mucus, turning a viral infection into a bacterial one that may require antibiotics.

Allergies

Allergic rhinitis (hay fever) is a major contributor to both acute and chronic sinusitis. When you're exposed to allergens like pollen, dust mites, pet dander, or mould, your immune system triggers inflammation in the nasal passages. This swelling can block the sinus openings and create the perfect environment for sinusitis to develop. If you find your sinus problems are seasonal or linked to specific environments, allergies may be playing a significant role.

Nasal Polyps

Nasal polyps are soft, painless, non-cancerous growths that develop on the lining of the nasal passages or sinuses. They result from chronic inflammation and, when large enough, can physically block the sinuses and nasal airflow. Polyps are commonly associated with chronic sinusitis, asthma, and aspirin sensitivity. They tend to recur, which is why ongoing management is often necessary.

Deviated Nasal Septum

The nasal septum is the thin wall of cartilage and bone that divides your two nostrils. Ideally, it sits right in the centre — but in many people, it leans to one side (a "deviated septum"). This can narrow one or both nasal passages, impair drainage, and make a person more susceptible to recurrent sinus infections. A deviated septum may be present from birth or result from a facial injury.

Recognising the Symptoms

Sinusitis can present with a range of symptoms, and their severity varies from person to person. Here are the most common signs to watch for:

Facial Pain and Pressure

One of the hallmark symptoms of sinusitis is a dull, aching pressure or pain in the face — particularly around the cheeks, forehead, eyes, and bridge of the nose. The location of the pain often corresponds to which sinuses are affected. This discomfort tends to worsen when you bend forward or lie down.

Nasal Congestion and Discharge

A blocked or stuffy nose is almost universal with sinusitis. You may also notice thick nasal discharge that is yellow or green in colour — a sign that your immune system is fighting an infection. Breathing through your nose may feel difficult or impossible during a flare-up.

Headache

Sinus headaches are caused by the build-up of pressure within the blocked sinuses. They typically present as a deep, constant ache in the forehead, cheekbones, or the bridge of the nose. Unlike migraines, sinus headaches are usually accompanied by other sinus symptoms and tend to worsen with sudden head movements.

Loss of Smell (and Taste)

Inflammation and congestion in the nasal passages can impair your sense of smell (anosmia) and, consequently, your sense of taste. This is often one of the more frustrating symptoms for patients, as it affects the enjoyment of food and can persist even after other symptoms have resolved.

Post-Nasal Drip

When excess mucus drains down the back of the throat rather than out through the nose, it's called post-nasal drip. This can cause a persistent sore throat, a tickling cough (especially at night), a hoarse voice, and a feeling of needing to constantly clear your throat.

Fever

A low-grade fever may accompany acute sinusitis, particularly when a bacterial infection is present. A high or persistent fever, however, warrants prompt medical attention as it may indicate a more serious complication.

When Should You See a Doctor?

Many mild cases of sinusitis resolve on their own within a week or two. However, you should seek medical advice if:

  • Your symptoms last longer than 10 days without improvement
  • You have a high fever (above 38.5°C / 101.3°F)
  • Your symptoms improve and then suddenly worsen (a "double worsening" pattern)
  • You experience severe facial pain, swelling around the eyes, or vision changes
  • You have a stiff neck or severe headache
  • You've had recurrent sinus infections (three or more per year)
  • Over-the-counter treatments are not providing relief

These symptoms — especially swelling around the eyes, vision changes, or a stiff neck — can signal rare but serious complications such as orbital cellulitis or meningitis. Don't hesitate to seek urgent care in these situations.

Treatment Options

The right treatment for sinusitis depends on its cause, severity, and duration. Here's an overview of the options available:

Home Remedies and Self-Care

For mild or early-stage sinusitis, simple home measures can provide meaningful relief and support recovery:

  • Steam inhalation — breathing in warm, moist air helps loosen mucus and ease congestion. A bowl of hot water with a towel over your head, or a warm shower, can work well.
  • Saline nasal rinses — using a neti pot or saline spray flushes out mucus and irritants, reduces inflammation, and keeps the nasal passages moist.
  • Warm compresses — applying a warm, damp cloth over your face can help relieve sinus pressure and pain.
  • Staying hydrated — drinking plenty of fluids helps thin mucus secretions, making them easier to drain.
  • Rest and elevation — getting adequate rest and sleeping with your head slightly elevated can reduce congestion and promote drainage.

Medications

Depending on the cause and severity of your sinusitis, your doctor may recommend or prescribe:

  • Decongestants — available as nasal sprays or oral tablets, these reduce swelling in the nasal passages. Note: nasal decongestant sprays should not be used for more than three consecutive days to avoid rebound congestion.
  • Nasal corticosteroid sprays — these are the cornerstone of treatment for both acute and chronic sinusitis. They reduce inflammation in the nasal lining and are safe for long-term use.
  • Antihistamines — if allergies are contributing to your sinusitis, antihistamines can help control the allergic response and reduce nasal inflammation.
  • Antibiotics — prescribed only when a bacterial infection is confirmed or strongly suspected. Viral sinusitis does not respond to antibiotics, so they are not a first-line treatment for every case.
  • Pain relievers — paracetamol or ibuprofen can help manage facial pain, headache, and fever.

When Surgery Is Needed

Surgery is considered when chronic sinusitis does not respond to prolonged medical treatment, or when a structural problem is identified as the root cause. The most common procedure is Functional Endoscopic Sinus Surgery (FESS) — a minimally invasive technique in which a thin, flexible camera (endoscope) is used to remove blockages, open up the sinus drainage pathways, and, if necessary, remove polyps or correct a deviated septum.

FESS is performed under general anaesthesia and is generally well-tolerated. Most patients experience significant improvement in symptoms and quality of life following surgery. Your ENT specialist will discuss whether surgery is appropriate for your specific situation and what to expect during recovery.

Prevention Tips: Keeping Your Sinuses Healthy

While not every case of sinusitis can be prevented, there are practical steps you can take to reduce your risk and support long-term sinus health:

  • Manage your allergies proactively — work with your doctor to identify your triggers and keep them under control with appropriate medication or immunotherapy.
  • Wash your hands regularly — most acute sinusitis begins with a viral cold. Good hand hygiene is one of the most effective ways to reduce your exposure.
  • Use a humidifier — keeping indoor air adequately moist, especially during dry seasons or in air-conditioned environments, helps prevent the nasal passages from drying out.
  • Avoid cigarette smoke and air pollutants — smoke and environmental irritants inflame the nasal lining and impair the natural mucus-clearing mechanism of the sinuses.
  • Stay hydrated — drinking enough water throughout the day keeps mucus thin and flowing freely.
  • Use saline nasal rinses regularly — particularly during cold and allergy season, a daily saline rinse can help clear irritants and keep the nasal passages healthy.
  • Treat colds promptly — don't ignore a cold that seems to be lingering or worsening. Early intervention can prevent a viral infection from progressing to bacterial sinusitis.

A Final Word

Sinusitis is incredibly common, but that doesn't mean you have to simply put up with it. Whether you're dealing with a one-off acute infection or a long-standing chronic condition, effective treatment is available — and the right care can make a real difference to how you feel day to day.

If you're struggling with persistent sinus symptoms, we encourage you to book a consultation with our ENT team. We'll take the time to understand your history, identify any underlying causes, and create a personalised treatment plan to help you breathe easier — literally.
